Introduction 
This guide is intended to present the users of Priority with the basic knowledge needed to 
begin working with the system. It explains the structure of menus, forms, reports , and 
programs; how to work with them; how to receive explanations and help from within the 
system; and the like. It does not, however, explain the functional use of the software. That 
is, it will answer such questions as "How do I add a new document ?" or "How do I search for 
and display data? ", but not "Which form do I use to enter a sales invoice?" or "How do I 
view warehouse balances? "

Priority Home Page 
Overview  
Welcome to Priority. As soon as you enter the system, you will see  the Home Page. 
Here you can:  
• Search Priority for all your data, such as customers, parts , and documents, or menu 
entities, such as forms and programs . 
• Access your frequently used forms and programs  quickly. 
• Change the settings, such as the system’s  language or company.  
• Catch up with changes made in your documents and chat with your colleagues  using 
the system’s chat . 
• View visual representations  of your data in portlets.  
This section introduces you to each element on the home page .

Entity Search 
Use the Entity Search to locate any form, program, and report in the system by its title or 
part of the title.  
Open the search box by clicking the magnifying glass icon on the left side of the Priority 
Menu bar. 
 
To search for an entity: 
1. In the Menu Search field, type in a term or part of a term. The search will be 
case sensitive unless you check the Ignore Case checkbox. 
2. Click the Search button or press Enter. All entity titles that contain the term will 
appear in the window. 
Entity Search

• Click the Run button to activate the entity directly.  
• Click the Help button to display the entity’s Help description.  
• Click the More button to access further options, such as: 
▪ Copy Link (to copy the entity together with a link to it) . 
▪ Path - View the entity's menu path. 
▪ Add the entity to the Home Page .

Priority Search Bar 
The Priority Search Bar is a powerful search engine which you can use to find your data in 
the system. You enter a search term, such as the name of a customer or an order number 
and the search engine will quickly find a single document, or if there are many documents with 
the same search term, you can open a search results form, where the documents can be viewed, 
conveniently sorted by document type with the number of results per type. 
The Priority Search Bar is located under the Priority Menu on the home page, or at the top 
of any form. 
 
Priority Search Bar 
 
Example 
You would like to find the purchase order you worked on yesterday for a specific 
vendor: 
1. In the Priority Search Bar, type in the vendor’s name or part of it (e.g., Da). 
2. As you type, the results appear. Click on a result to open it. 
Search results 
3. If the document does not appear, click  on the Search Bar to open a search results 
form. This form shows all the documents, forms and reports that contain the search 
name. 
The left side displays the type and number of documents found, while the right side 
displays the list of documents.

7. You can return to the search results form from the Active Screens list.  
Search Using Special Characters  
In the Priority Search Bar, you can use special characters to focus your search results. 
Type Example 
"… …" 
Use quotation marks to search for an exact phrase.  
For example: 
• "World Wide Sounds " 
+ 
Use the plus sign (+) to indicate that the term must appear in the searched 
document. 
For example: 
• world + wide (this shows all the results with the term wide) 
• world +"wide sounds" 
- 
Use the minus sign (-) to exclude a term from the search.  
For example:
13:
Cat.No LBI2300661 9 Ver.01 – October 01, 2023 
 
• sounds -world (this shows all the results with the term sounds, but 
without the term world) 
Make sure that there is no space between the minus sign and the excluded 
search term. 
$ 
Use $ to search for a specific  word only, without additional forms of the word.  
For example: 
• $sound (this shows all the results with the term sound, but without 
returning documents with  the term sounds) 
Home Page Widgets 
Your Priority system comes with a set of widgets on your home page. These widgets are 
essentially shortcuts to the system entities that you use on a regular basis , such as the Sales 
Orders form. You can delete the existing widgets and add new widgets according to your 
needs. You can rearrange the  order of the widgets by dragging them around using your 
mouse. 
To add a widget to your Home Page, either: 
• On the Home Page, c lick the   icon to open the Add to Home Page  window. Find 
an entity via the Search window and click Add, or, 
• On the Priority Menu, navigate to an entity, then, right-click the entity and select 
Add to Home Page . 
To remove widgets from the home page : 
• Hover with your mouse over the upper -right corner of a widget and click the More 
Options  icon, and then click Delete.

Portlets 
You can display up -to-date company data in graph form (e.g., open sales orders, purchase 
orders, profit and loss) in portlets on your home page.  
To add a portlet to the home page:  
• Click the  icon and choose a topic in the window that opens. The portlet will 
then appear on your home page.  
• Hover with your mouse over the upper -left corner of the portlet and then click the 
three dots that appear. Choose one of the following options:  
• Select Choose Dates to select the period for which to display the data (in relevant 
portlets). 
• Select Open in New Window  to open the portlet in a window outside of the home 
page. 
• Select Delete to delete the portlet from your home page.  
Note 
• You must have the BI module installed to display portlets on your home page.  
• To ensure that invoice portlets display up -to-date data, you need to run the Prep 
for Inv. Analysis (BI) program (for customer invoices and purchase invoices) daily. 
The programs can be run at fixed times by the Tabula Task Scheduler.

Forms 
Forms are where you work  and have access to all your data . They are where you open new 
documents and update existing ones.  
For example, you can open new orders in the Sales Orders form, or you can look for orders 
that you or your colleagues have opened.  
You can open each form in various views. Each view is recommended for a specific activity 
or action. The view you choose has no effect on the content of the record. The only 
difference is in the way the data appears . 
All the views have many basic elements in common, described below.  
Later, we will highlight the special characteristics of each view and when to use it.  
 
Form List view 
 
A form has the following elements : 
 
Priority Menu and Priority Search Bar – These are the same elements as in the 
home page, including the search bar. For more information, see page 4 and page 7. 
 
Form Header – Contains the form name and the buttons, icons and actions that 
are relevant to all forms, as well as actions specific to the current form.

about that on page 77. 
 
Splitter – The splitter separates the upper-level and subforms. Drag the splitter up 
or down to show more records (the Splitter only appears when you are in List 
view).

Form Title – Here you can see the action that you are currently doing – adding a

How to Save a New Record 
To streamline your day-to-day work, your data is automatically saved  by Priority.  
Any details that you add or update are saved immediately by the system as you move from 
one record to another, when you move from an upper -level to a subform, or when you close 
or exit a form.  
You will know that the new record was saved because the form heading changes.  
When you first add a new record, the form heading shows:  
 
The moment a record is saved automatically (for example, if you move to a subform), the 
form heading change s and now shows the record number:   
 
When you add further information to the record, or change existing information, you will 
see a caption in the form heading with the text Saving changes ... or All changes were 
saved. There is no need to look for this caption or even to wait for it. You can simply 
continue working. 
When you are finished working on the record, click the Back to List button or the minimizer 
icon  on the top right side of the form. This will return you to the Sales Orders form, 
where you can see the list of  relevant sales orders (this is discussed in more depth in the 
View Records in List View section on page 30).

Drill Down Icons 
After drilling down to the relevant form, you can locate the value, select it and return to the 
form where you were working. 
Example 
You opened a new sales order, and you want it to contain a bundle of products that 
were already defined in the system.  
1. In the Sales Order form, in the Price tab, place your cursor in the Product Bundle field 
and start typing. A list opens showing relevant product bundles. 
 
But, you decide that you want to check which products are in a product bundle before 
you choose it. And this information does not appear in the list. So, you need to drill 
down to the form that contains the source data. 
2. Click on the list icon next to the field to drill down to the Product Bundles 
(Quotes/Orders) form.

the Action buttons or the Actions list ). The latter topic was discussed above; the current 
section thus deals with running programs and reports from a menu.

Reports present data on an HTML page in an organized manner that makes viewing easy.

Generating a report gives you the ability to send to an external recipient, for example, a tax

of company activities, for example, giving users instant access to critical data, and more 
such as: 
• viewing availability of rental parts in dates you specify  
• viewing receipts/returns for a vendor in specific dates, together with invoices  
• viewing price lists recorded for vendors

Report Format 
There are several different types of reports: standard, tables, graphs, documents, BI reports, 
and charts. All reports presented as HTML pages open in your web browser.
94:

Example of a standard HTML report 
Tables differ from standard reports in two major areas. First, they summarize information. 
Whereas reports provide a great deal of specific data over several pages, tables succinctly 
provide a broad picture of the situation. Second, tables can provide info rmation (e.g., sum 
totals) per row and field).  
There are several types of graphs: bar, line, area, and tree graphs. The key to each bar, area 
or line appears below its appropriate scale. In the key that appears below the graph, a bar is 
denoted by a shaded rectangle, an area by a shaded triangle, and a line graph by a line.  
Tree graphs illustrate ratios between parent data and their children, between children and 
their children, and so on down to the lowest level. The ratio appears to the right of the line 
that connects parent to child. A tree that is wider than the screen will be divided among 
several pages. The number of the page on which e ach portion of the graph is continued 
appears within the parent's box, at its right edge. All graphs are printed in landscape 
orientation. 
Documents are a special type of report whose printout is sent to customers, vendors or 
other external recipients (e.g., customer invoices). A standard HTML format is provided for 
all documents, which can be changed using the Design Report tool, discussed in detail under 
Personalize Your Priority , page 108. You can also design Word templates for documents as 
discussed in detail under The Word Document Generator, page 161. 
On BI reports and charts, see page 96.

Drill Down Within BI Reports 
Begin by selecting the desired Dimensions format (click the  icon). The One -dimensional 
format displays each level of data by itself. That is, once you select a dimension by which to 
slice the data in a screen, the selected cross -section appears alone. You can then continue 
to drill down within the data until you reach the cross -section that you want. To view data 
for the previous level, you need to return to the previous screen (e.g., by clicking the back 
arrow).  
In contrast, the Multi -dimensional format enables you to drill down and view different 
cross-sections of the data in a single screen, organized within a hierarchical tree. When 
drilling down within the data, each cross -section is sliced by the selected dim ension. The 
corresponding node in the tree is expanded accordingly (and can later be collapsed). This 
enables you to view values for the entire population as well as zoom in on specific cross -
sections without losing global context.  
After selecting the desired Dimensions format, you can begin selecting the cross -section of 
data to be displayed by clicking All. For example, in Sales Opportunity Analysis (BI), the 
input screen that appears lets you choose to break down the data by customer number, part 
number and territory. If you are working in One -dimensional format, this input screen also 
contains Filter by and Filter Except for  options that let you view or eliminate all data that 
match a designated pattern (specified in the Use Pattern field). Alternatively, you can 
choose to total all data in the defined cross -section, by choosing the Total by or Total 
Except for options. 
For example, to eliminate data for customers whose customer number begins with the 
letter T, select Customer Number  to break down data by customer, choose the Filter Except 
for option and record the value "T*" in the Use Pattern field.

to an external program such as Excel .

Parameter Input  
Most programs and reports require user input. Among other things, this input determines 
the records that will be affected by the program or the records that will be displayed in the 
report. 
User input is designated via the pop -up parameter input screen, which is comprised of one 
or more fields, some of which have both right -hand and left-hand input windows. The right -
hand window is for inputting values; the left -hand one is for selecting operators (where 
relevant). 
Use the mouse, Tab key or  arrow keys to move between input fields. Do not press Enter 
until you have finished specifying all input.  
When an input screen opens, it displays the parameters that were used the last time the 
program was run. If you are not interested in using these parameters, click the Clear button 
and enter new ones.  
Parameters must be designated in at least one field in each input screen. This is why the 
first input field in each screen features the default “*” wildcard, even though an empty 
input field achieves the same effect (i.e., inputs all records).  
The exception to this rule is an input field that requires a single value (e.g., From Date, To 
Date), in which case input is mandatory and blank fields are invalid.

Priority’s Automation s Hub 
Priority provides many tools to  help you streamline your workflow and personalize the 
system to the work and business needs of every customer.  This includes tools to automate 
and quicken routine work processes and recurring tasks.  
You can find these tools in the automation s hub, accessible from every form in the system, 
under the  button in the form header.  
 
Using the tools described in this section, users can:  
• Define macros for recurring tasks (see the Macros chapter that follows this 
introduction). 
• Create messages to be sent by email or shown in the system when certain conditions 
are met (see the Business Rules chapter on page  148). 
• Update data in fields automatically when certain conditions are met  (see the Autofill 
Rules chapter on page 158). 
Macros 
Macros is one of Priority’s most powerful tools in that it accelerates recurring tasks in the 
system. Using macros, the system can perform a sequence of actions instead of the user 
manually performing them. For instance, if you need to delete a bunch of records one after 
the other, or select a group of lines  and enter the data repeatedly, in one form or in several 
forms. These kinds of actions do not need to take up much time or effort. Instead, the 
system can do them automatically, with the click of a button.
147:
Cat.No LBI2300661 143 Ver.01 – October 01, 2023 
 
To define these kinds of tasks , you only need to record them once. By recording them , you 
are showing the system what you want it to do for you automatically. Each stroke and click 
is recorded. Every time you run the macro, the system will perform this sequence of actions 
by itself. 
Creating a New Macro  
1. Enter the form in which you want to start recording the macro.  
2. From the form header, select the Automations button, then Macros > Record 
Macro. 
3. In the dialog that appears:  
4. Assign a name to the macro. This name will later be used to run the macro, so 
try to make it as intuitive as possible.  
5. If you want, define the macro a shortcut key. Each time you select ALT + the 
number you choose, the macro will run. If you do not choose a shortcut key, you 
can always run the macro from the Automations hub. 
6. Toggle the Repeat button if you want the sequence of recorded actions to 
repeat itself over and over on several records. If you want to sequence of 
actions to perform itself on one record only, leave the Repeat button off. 
7. Select the Start Recording button. 
8. At this point you see two buttons on the lower right side of the screen.  
9. The blinking red button means that the system is recording your actions - 
keystrokes and mouse clicks.  
10. Once you have finished the entire sequence of actions, select the blue 
button. 
11. After you finish recording the macro (by selecting the blue button, or 
Automations > Macros, select Stop Recording), the macro is saved and you can 
use it from here on.  
 
 Note:  Recursive Macros  
When you toggle the Repeat button and record a macro, the sequence of actions will 
repeat itself over and over. When you run the macro defined as recursive (on repeat), 
the sequence of actions will run on the records or documents shown in the form, or 
until the number of repeats reaches the maximum (defined in the RECURSIVEMACRO 
system constant).
148:
Cat.No LBI2300661 144 Ver.01 – October 01, 2023 
 
Examples of Recording Different Types of Macros  
These are the three main types of macros that you can record and run:  
1. A macro that updates data, in one form, without repeating itself , when you 
want to open the same type of document in different scenarios, and you want 
to update the same data each time.  
2. A recursive macro  (on repeat) in which you want to update data, in one form. 
Use this macro w hen you want to update the same data in a bunch of 
documents, for example, update the supply date in several lines, select the 
Authorize checkbox in several lines, change the status in a few documents, or 
even delete a few lines). We will show an example of this lower down on the 
page. 
3. A recursive cross-form macro (on repeat), to run a few actions and/or update 
data, in several forms . Use this when you want to open new documents   or copy 
data from one form to another, for example. See an example on page  146. 
4. We will present examples for the 2 most frequent ways macros are used (2 & 3 
from above).

want to update the due date in each line of the order items.

this data to each line in the form  automatically. The macro you define now will be 
useful for each time you need to copy data from a field in one line to the same field in 
every line under it.  
1. Enter Sales Orders to the relevant order, and then enter the subform Order Items. 
2. In the Due Date field, manually change the due date to the new date. 
3. Go down one line, so that the cursor is in the second line of the Due Date field in the 
Order Items subform.

Define Business Rules 
Defining business rules in Priority can help streamline your work so that you do not have to 
track your documents and actions, but you can just receive the relevant messages you need 
to pay attention to.  
Business Rules let you define any of the following:  
● Error message, which prevents you from completing the attempted action based on 
defined conditions  
● Warning, which alerts you to potential consequences that may result from the 
completed action

● Email, which can be accompanied by an attached printout of the current record and 
sent to a user, group, email address or contact  
● Text message, which can be sent to any user, group, mobile phone number or 
contact 
● Push notification, which can be sent to a Priority Mobile user  
● Task, which can be accompanied by an attached printout of the current record and 
assigned to any user.  
● User verification, which can affirm that the user signed into the system is actually 
the user updating a specific , sensitive field, to prevent a situation in which a 
computer is left open and another employee may try to update a field  for which 
they do not have permissions , (at this time not supported for  mobile or with an 
external ID).

The Word Document Generator 
The Word Document Generator lets you produce an infinite variety of form -based 
documents that display data for document printouts (entities with the   icon) usually sent 
to a customer or vendor, such as a sales order confirmation . These template -based 
documents display only those data fields you want to appear, exported to a Word file from 
the system via XML tags.   
Each XML tag represents a field from a specific Priority form. When such a tag is implanted 
in a Word template, it can receive whatever value is currently stored in that field. For 
example, the Document field tag will receive the document number of the form record 
whose data are sent to the template.  
Note 
To use XML templates, you must have Microsoft Office 2013 or higher installed on your 
workstation, and you must be authorized for document design (in the User Permissions  
form > Permissions tab). 
There are two aspects involved in generating Word documents from system data: creating 
Word templates and sending a specific form record to the template to generate a 
document.

A container is a dynamic text c omponent that lets you define a content component that will 
only be inserted  into a Word template, when  a predefined rule is met. So, for example, you 
can define a container that is inserted into orders for an electronic device (e.g., a new 
smartphone) that includes an offer to purchase an extended warranty .

the document based on the definitions of the container.

language by clicking the English tab. The message will be sent in the language from 
which the template was defined.

list of all field titles in the opened form. You can use this list to add a field 
representing the field’s current value into the message.

The Label Generator 
This feature lets you create labels from a form, using data that is taken automatically from 
designated form fields. The Label Generator program automatically populates the Label 
Preparation Work Area  form with values from the designated form and/or its upper -level 
forms. Labels that are prepared this way can then be edited in the Label Preparation Work 
Area and/or printed.  
Note 
To make full use of these features, you must have a label printer and label printing 
program, such as BarTender  or NiceLabel installed on your computer or cloud solution. 
For details, see the Help for the Print Labels program, located at the following menu 
path: Inventory → Label Printing. 
Design Labels for a Form 
Use the Label Generator  form and its subform to design a label for any form, based on the 
values of the designated form fields. When the Label Generator is run from that form, each 
variable will be populated automatically with the value of the linked form field. 
In the following example, practice designing a label for sold parts.  
Example 
1. Enter the Label Generator form and search for the Order Items form. 
2. Select "Orders" as the Type of Label and indicate the Quantity of Labels to be prepared 
for each order item. 
3. Move to the Label Parameters subform. 
4. In the line for the first Parameter, select "Part Number" as the Form Field. 
5. Move to the next line and select "Unit Price" as the Form Field to be linked to the 
second Parameter. 
6. In the third line, mark the Upper-level Form Col field to link the third parameter to a 
field that appears in the upper-level Sales Orders form and then select "Order" as the 
Form Field. 
7. Do the same in the fourth line, and select "Date" as the Form Field.

Once you have designed a label for a form, you can use it to generate an unlimited number 
of labels from individual form records. When a label is designed for a subform (as in the 
previous example), you can choose between preparing labels for the record on which the 
cursor is located and preparing them for all displayed records in the subform. You can also 
choose between editing the label data in the Label Preparation Work Area  or printing the 
labels immediately.

BPM Flow Charts  
This section contains instructions for setting up Business Process Management (BPM) flow 
charts, easy-to-use graphic interfaces through which you can control and monitor the 
progress of most basic system processes.  
A special type of program opens a flow chart, which you can use to manage business 
processes. In this friendly graphic interface, you can define statuses and link them together 
into defined sequences by means of paths. Both statuses and the paths that link them may 
be assigned user -defined rules that trigger a variety of automatic features when certain 
conditions are met. The end product is a sophisticated workflow mechanism that optimizes 
the efficiency of the process.  
Each major document in a defined work process has its own flow chart.  
If you are working with statuses for the first time, each chart opens to display the default 
statuses predefined for that document. You can update the default flow chart by adding 
statuses, assigning paths, and creating rules that reflect the company workflow.
186:
Cat.No LBI2300661 182 Ver.01 – October 01, 2023 
 
 
The BPM flow chart for vendor price quotes 
Note 
Flow charts have a Tool Tray in the lower left corner, with shortcuts for various actions. 
There are also certain commands in the Options Menu that are only available when in a 
BPM flow chart, while other commands are disabled.  
The Initial Status 
Each BPM flow chart has a default initial status (usually Draft) that is pre -defined. When you 
right-click on that status, the Default Initial Status  line is marked. This is the status that is 
assigned to any newly opened document.  
In addition, you can define any number of statuses as a permitted initial status. As a result, 
when users open a new document, even though a default status appears, they can change it 
to any permitted status without having to leave and reenter the line.  
To define a status in this way,  
• Right-click it in the flow chart and select Permitted Initial Status . 
Create Paths Between Statuses 
Paths determine the available options for moving a transaction document from one status 
to another. Represented by arrows in the flow chart, paths reflect the order in which the 
various processes in a transaction must take place. When attached to rules, th ey may also 
determine the conditions under which a status may change, as well as the way a transaction 
is processed when it reaches the next status.

Note 
To temporarily disable the paths between statuses in the form in which you are 
working (though properties and rules remain in effect), from the Options Menu, click 
Edit and select Disable Path Definitions.  
Example 
To create a path from one status to another, perform the following steps:  
1. Use the Entity Search feature (page 6) to find and run the BPM Flow Chart – Sales 
Orders. 
2. Use the mouse to drag the statuses so that they appear in a clear hierarchy on the 
chart. 
3. To add a path between statuses, do one of the following: 
• Click  on the Tool Tray.  
• Right-click an empty area on the flow chart and select Draw Path. 
• From the Options Menu, click Edit and select Draw Path. 
4. Use the mouse to draw a line from the Draft status to the In Progress status. 
5. Draw two paths from the In Progress status (one to the Confirmed status and one to 
the Canceled status) and a path from the Confirmed status to the Completed status.  
6. To save the changes, click  on the Tool Tray; or from the Options Menu, click File 
and select Save BPM.

Define Rules for Statuses 
BPM lets you monitor workflow by setting up rules that activate system responses when 
certain conditions are met. You can set up automatic email, text messages or push messages 
for Priority Mobile whenever a document receives a certain status, remains in a certain 
status overlong or receives a date that falls within a certain time period. Other options 
include re-assigning the document to a higher -ranking employee or opening a task. You can 
use rules in tandem with data fields to define precise conditions for the activ ation of these 
responses (e.g., the size of the transaction, the importance of the customer).  
Note 
Text messaging (available in Priority Pro) must be purchased in a separate module.  
You can also define a rule such that email, text messages or Push Notification for Priority 
Mobile are sent to an entire us er group rather than an individual user. In this case, the 
document will be assigned to the first user in the group, who can then assign the document 
to another user in that group.

Note 
Groups are an important tool in Priority, with applications in customer relations, 
mailing and business rules.  
To define a group:  
• Enter the Groups form, found in the Contacts menu of the Office Management  
module. 
Note: This is a multi -company form. Any revisions to it affect all companies.  
• Specify a Group Name and Description. If you are defining a group for mailing 
purposes, use the To/Cc/Bcc field to determine in which mode each member of the 
group will receive the message.  
• Enter the Users in Group subform and list the users that belong to it.  
• To determine the group hierarchy (e.g., for when automatic mail is sent to the first 
person in the group), use the  Sort field. 
• If you are defining a group for mailing purposes, you can enter the E-mail Addresses 
subform and add external email recipients to your group.  
Note 
When using BPM to assign a document to a user in the group, if the first user is not 
available, the document is automatically assigned to the next person in the group. The 
availability of the user is determined by defined employee hours and absences.

Run the Tabula Task Scheduler 
The sending of time -based messages will only work if the Tabula Task Scheduler  is running 
and has been set up properly. The scheduler scans all active documents for any defined time 
limits and activates the notification process when it finds an expired limit. It also sends a 
daily error report for any time -activated changes (e.g., status, assigned user) that have 
malfunctioned. 
Basic instructions for setting up the scheduler can be downloaded from the Priority web 
site. In addition, the system administrator needs to make sure that :

• An outgoing mail application other than Outlook is defined on the workstation 
where the scheduler is installed.  
• Send reminders about document status  program is marked as Active in the Define 
TTS Tasks form. 
BPM Error Reports 
An escalation rule may trigger a change of user or status when such a change is not 
permitted in the document, based on defined form logic. For example, a Customer Shipment 
may not receive the “Final” status if it has not yet been itemized. In such a case,  the form 
presents an error message; however, since escalation rules are applied automatically when 
the form is closed, error messages are not possible. Instead, Priority provides a report called 
BPM – History of Errors  (menu path: System Management → System Maintenance → 
Periodic Maintenance → BPM Maintenance) to document any actions that fail for this 
reason. 
You can set the scheduler to send the error report to a designated email address at regular 
intervals, where it can be checked routinely for failed actions. To do so:  
• Open the Define TTS Tasks form. Move to the task named “Send Reminders – error 
log by email (change the address)”.  
• In the Command field, move to the end of the command line and replace the phrase 
“any_email_address@mycompany.com” with the desired address.  
Define Rules for Paths 
You can further optimize your workflow by assigning rules to the paths between statuses, 
rather than to the statuses themselves. Path rules let you assign behaviors to a status when 
it is reached from a specific preceding one. Path rules also let you designate which users or 
groups of users are permitted to move a document from one particular status to another. As 
with status rules, this rule can be made conditional on document data.  
Note 
Paths that have been assigned rules display a small red circle in the middle of the line.  
Example 
Let’s return to your sales order flow chart:  
1. Reopen the flow chart. Right-click the path between the Confirmed status and the 
Canceled status, and select Rules.
199:
Cat.No LBI2300661 195 Ver.01 – October 01, 2023 
 
2. In the Do the following section, select Allow status change. In the third text box, select 
any username (except yours). 
3. Click OK. The new rule establishes that once a sales order has been confirmed; it can 
only be canceled by the specified user. 
4. Save and exit the flow chart. 
5. Search for a sales order and assign it the Confirmed status. Now try to change the 
status to Canceled. 
If you want to disable a particular rule (for either a status or a path) you can remove the 
check mark from the Active option in the Define Rules dialogue box or in the  List of Rules 
dialogue box. 
Note 
To view all rules created for a status or path, use the List of BPM Rules form in the BPM 
Maintenance menu or click the More button in the List of Rules dialogue box, and select 
Report. 
Change a Status 
To change an existing status, right -click the status and select the desired command from the 
pop-up menu: 
Note 
The same commands can be accessed from the Options Menu > Edit menu. 
• Rules - Opens the Define rules… dialogue box, in which you define status rules.  
• Properties - Opens the Properties of… dialogue box, in which you define attributes 
of the status, such as whether a document in this status can be updated or will be 
included in your to do list.  
• Set Color - Lets you select a background color for the status (as displayed in the flow 
chart). This color will also display as a colored icon next to the record's status field 
when in focus and single record mode.  
• Rename - Lets you choose a new name for the status.  
• Record Remark - Lets you enter a brief remark for the status that will appear as a 
tooltip in the flow chart.  
• Delete - Removes the status and its paths from the flow chart (and makes it inactive, 
that is, the status will not appear in drop-down lists).
200:
Cat.No LBI2300661 196 Ver.01 – October 01, 2023 
 
Note 
Any status that you delete is stored in memory together with all other statuses that are 
not in use in the current flow chart. These statuses appear in the Select Status window 
whenever you add a new status.  
A document may be assigned numerous statuses. To keep your flow chart easily 
comprehensible, special care must be taken both in the color -coding of statuses, and in 
their physical grouping. For example, you may want to situate all statuses that involve 
closing, canceling , or freezing the document at the bottom of the screen, and color them 
red. In addition, you may find it useful to enter a brief remark for each status, which will 
appear when you hover over the status in the flow chart.  
Add New Statuses  
To add a new status to the flow chart , do one of the following:  
• Click  on the Tool Tray.  
• Right-click an empty area on the flow chart and select Add Status. 
• From the Options Menu, click Edit and select Add Status. 
• The Select Status window will appear. Click the New button. 
• A dialogue box called Properties of the New Status  opens. Enter a Name for the 
new status, and mark the appropriate status properties.  
View a History of BPM Flow Chart Changes  
Use the BPM – Track Changes form or report in the BPM Maintenance menu to view all 
changes made in any BPM chart (e.g., new status, path deleted, new rule, change in status 
or status attributes).  
Copy a BPM Flow Chart  
You copy an existing flow chart to a clipboard, from which it can pasted into another 
company or even to another installation of Priority. To do so: 
• Open the flow chart.  
• From the Options Menu, click Edit and select Copy Flow Chart.  
• Open the same chart in the target company.  
• From the Options Menu, click Edit and select Paste Flow Chart .
201:
Cat.No LBI2300661 197 Ver.01 – October 01, 2023 
 
Note 
You can also paste the flow chart into a Notepad file and save it as a backup. The saved 
text can then be copied and pasted into a target chart using the Paste Flow Chart  
option.
